section.title: The Broken Jar and the Prophet's Suffering
section.sectionRationale: A second sign act follows the potter's house: Jeremiah smashes a hardened clay jar before the elders to picture a judgment that, unlike soft clay, can never be reworked. The cost of that word falls immediately on the messenger himself, as Pashhur has him beaten and put in the stocks, and the section ends with Jeremiah's rawest confession, cursing the very day of his birth.
section.thesis: The broken jar shows a judgment now so hardened it cannot be reshaped, and the suffering that follows for Jeremiah, first public humiliation, then a private crisis of faith, shows what it costs a true prophet to carry a word the world will not hear.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: the jar shattered beyond repair in the Valley of Slaughter, the same word proclaimed in the temple court, Pashhur's violence against the prophet, and Jeremiah's confession, from fire in his bones to cursing his own birth.

units.label: The broken jar
units.note: a judgment that cannot be reshaped

verses.text: This is what the LORD says: “Go and buy a clay {{baqbuq|jar}} from a potter. Take some of the elders of the people and leaders of the priests,

and go out to the Valley of Ben-hinnom near the entrance of the Potsherd Gate. 
Proclaim there the words I speak to you,

saying, ‘Hear the word of the LORD, O kings of Judah and residents of Jerusalem. This is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: I am going to bring such disaster on this place that the ears of all who hear of it will ring,

because they have abandoned Me and made this a foreign place. They have burned incense in this place to other gods that neither they nor their fathers nor the kings of Judah have ever known. They have filled this place with the blood of the innocent.
They have built high places to Baal on which to burn their children in the fire as offerings to Baal, something I never commanded or mentioned, nor did it even enter My mind.
So behold, the days are coming, declares the LORD, when this place will no longer be called Topheth or the Valley of Ben-hinnom, but the Valley of Slaughter.

And in this place I will ruin the plans of Judah and Jerusalem. I will make them fall by the sword before their enemies, by the hands of those who seek their lives, and I will give their carcasses as food to the birds of the air and the beasts of the earth.
I will make this city a desolation and an object of scorn. All who pass by will be appalled and will scoff at all her wounds.
I will make them eat the flesh of their sons and daughters, and they will eat one another's flesh in the siege and distress inflicted on them by their enemies who seek their lives.’
Then you are to shatter the jar in the presence of the men who accompany you,

and you are to proclaim to them that this is what the LORD of Hosts says: I will shatter this nation and this city, like one shatters a potter's jar that can never again be repaired. They will bury the dead in Topheth until there is no more room to bury them.
This is what I will do to this place and to its residents, declares the LORD. I will make this city like Topheth.

The houses of Jerusalem and the houses of the kings of Judah will be defiled like that place, Topheth, all the houses on whose rooftops they burned incense to all the host of heaven and poured out drink offerings to other gods.”
Then Jeremiah returned from Topheth, where the LORD had sent him to prophesy, and he stood in the courtyard of the house of the LORD and proclaimed to all the people,

“This is what the LORD of Hosts, the God of Israel, says: ‘Behold, I am about to bring on this city and on all the villages around it every disaster I have pronounced against them, because they have stiffened their necks so as not to heed My words.’”
When Pashhur the priest, the son of Immer and the chief official in the house of the LORD, heard Jeremiah prophesying these things,
he had Jeremiah the prophet beaten and put in the stocks at the Upper Gate of Benjamin, which was by the house of the LORD.
The next day, when Pashhur released Jeremiah from the stocks, Jeremiah said to him, “The LORD does not call you Pashhur, but {{magormissabib|Magor-missabib}}.

For this is what the LORD says: ‘I will make you a terror to yourself and to all your friends. They will fall by the sword of their enemies before your very eyes. And I will hand Judah over to the king of Babylon, and he will carry them away to Babylon and put them to the sword.

I will give away all the wealth of this city, all its products and valuables, and all the treasures of the kings of Judah, to their enemies. They will plunder them, seize them, and carry them off to Babylon.

And you, Pashhur, and all who live in your house, will go into captivity. You will go to Babylon, and there you will die and be buried, you and all your friends to whom you have prophesied these lies.’”

units.label: Jeremiah's confession
units.note: fire in the bones, and cursing his own birth

verses.text: You have deceived me, O LORD, and I was deceived. You have overcome me and prevailed. I am a laughingstock all day long; everyone mocks me.
For whenever I speak, I cry out; I proclaim violence and destruction. For the word of the LORD has become to me a reproach and derision all day long.

If I say, “I will not mention Him or speak any more in His name,” His message becomes a {{esh|fire}} burning in my heart, shut up in my bones, and I become weary of holding it in, and I cannot prevail.

For I have heard the whispering of many: “Terror is on every side! 
Report him; let us report him!” All my trusted friends watch for my fall: “Perhaps he will be deceived so that we may prevail against him 
and take our vengeance upon him.”

But the LORD is with me like a fearsome warrior. Therefore, my persecutors will stumble and will not prevail. Since they have not succeeded, they will be utterly put to shame, with an everlasting disgrace that will never be forgotten.
O LORD of Hosts, who examines the righteous, who sees the heart and mind, let me see Your vengeance upon them, for to You I have committed my cause.
Sing to the LORD! Praise the LORD! For He rescues the life of the needy from the hands of evildoers.
Cursed be the day I was born! May the day my mother bore me never be blessed.

Cursed be the man who brought my father the news, saying, “A son is born to you,” 
bringing him great joy.
May that man be like the cities that the LORD overthrew without compassion. May he hear an outcry in the morning and a battle cry at noon,

because he did not kill me in the womb so that my mother might have been my grave, 
and her womb forever enlarged.

Why did I come out of the womb to see only trouble and sorrow, 
and to end my days in shame?

baqbuq.gloss: A narrow-necked clay flask, named for the gurgling sound it makes when poured out. Unlike the soft, reworkable clay of the potter's wheel in chapter 18, this jar has already been fired hard; once shattered it can never be reshaped, a deliberate escalation from warning to finality.

magormissabib.gloss: Terror on every side, the new name Jeremiah gives Pashhur after his violence against the prophet. It turns Pashhur's own cruelty back on him as a prophecy of his fate, and the same phrase later becomes Jeremiah's own description of his persecutors' whispering, verse 10.

esh.gloss: Fire. Jeremiah describes God's word as a fire shut up in his bones that he cannot hold in even when he wants to stop speaking; it captures the involuntary, consuming compulsion of genuine prophetic calling, not a task he chose but a fire he cannot quench.

basic.context: Jeremiah buys a hardened clay jar and smashes it before the city's elders in the Valley of Ben-hinnom, a place so associated with child sacrifice to Baal that God renames it the Valley of Slaughter. Unlike the reworkable clay of chapter 18, this jar, once broken, stays broken, a sign that judgment has now become fixed. When Jeremiah proclaims this in the temple courtyard, the priest Pashhur has him beaten and locked in the stocks, and Jeremiah's response is some of the most anguished, honest prayer in all of Scripture, ending with him cursing the very day he was born.
basic.hermeneutics: These chapters are read as real history, an actual sign act and an actual arrest, not merely literary devices. The hardest part for modern readers is Jeremiah's raw language, 'You have deceived me, O LORD,' and his curse on his own birth; the honest answer is that Scripture includes this kind of anguished, unfiltered prayer without either excusing sin or hiding real spiritual crisis, and it does not end there, Jeremiah still proclaims God's word even in his lowest moment.
basic.originalLanguages: The jar, baqbuq, is fired hard clay, no longer reworkable like the soft clay of chapter 18, a deliberate escalation. Pashhur is renamed Magor-missabib, terror on every side, turning his cruelty into a prophecy against himself, and the same phrase later describes the whispering campaign against Jeremiah. And Jeremiah describes God's word as esh, fire, shut up in his bones, capturing a compulsion he cannot resist even when persecution makes him want to.
basic.typology: The broken, unmendable jar anticipates a judgment beyond the point of easy repentance, echoed later in the New Testament's warnings about hardened unbelief. Jeremiah's suffering for faithful speech, beaten and mocked for the word he carried, previews the far greater suffering of Christ, who was likewise struck, mocked, and rejected for speaking the truth of God.
basic.christ: Jeremiah's cry, 'I am a laughingstock all day long, everyone mocks me,' anticipates the mockery Christ endured on the cross, and his experience of a word he cannot hold in even under threat mirrors Christ's own resolute obedience to the Father's will despite anguish, seen also in Gethsemane. Yet where Jeremiah curses his own birth in despair, Christ presses through His own anguish to completion, 'It is finished,' showing the fuller obedience Jeremiah's honest struggle points toward.
basic.god: God is patient enough to keep sending warnings even to a hardened people, but just enough that some judgments do finally become fixed, like the broken jar. And God is present with His suffering messenger, not shielding Jeremiah from persecution but proving Himself 'a fearsome warrior' at his side even in the darkest hour of doubt.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 6:4-6 speaks of a hardness that becomes settled and resistant to repentance, echoing the unmendable jar. Matthew 27:29-31 shows Christ mocked much as Jeremiah was mocked for speaking truth. Job 3 contains a similarly raw curse on the day of birth, showing Jeremiah stands within a biblical tradition of honest lament. 1 Peter 4:12-14 encourages suffering for righteousness as something Christ Himself shared.
basic.oneStory: The broken jar and Jeremiah's suffering show that carrying God's true word in a hostile world is costly, sometimes to the point of anguish that dares to curse one's own birth. That costly faithfulness finds its full and final pattern in Christ, who bore mockery and suffering for speaking truth all the way to the cross, so that those who suffer for His name are never suffering alone.

context.literary: This is the second sign act in the sequence begun at the potter's house, moving from reworkable clay to a hardened jar that cannot be mended, a literary escalation from warning to finality. The narrative then pivots from public prophecy to personal suffering, pairing sign act with lived cost in a way unique to Jeremiah among the prophets.
context.historical: Ben-hinnom, where the jar is broken, was the valley where children had been sacrificed to Molech and Baal, later renamed Gehenna and used by Jesus as an image for final judgment. Pashhur held real institutional power as chief official of the temple, making his violence against Jeremiah an attack from the highest religious authority of the day.
context.canonical: Jeremiah's confessions, of which this is one of the most intense, are unique in the prophetic books for their unfiltered honesty before God, forming a bridge between the raw laments of the Psalms and the pattern of righteous suffering later fulfilled in Christ and modeled for believers in the epistles.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show, through both a dramatic sign act and Jeremiah's own suffering, that God's patience with a persistently unrepentant nation has limits, and that faithfully carrying God's word can cost the messenger dearly, even to the point of profound personal anguish.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The text describes Jeremiah's actual experience and his actual prayer; it does not prescribe cursing one's birth as a model devotional practice. It does model bringing the rawest, most honest anguish directly to God rather than suppressing it or hiding it from Him.

debates.question: Is Jeremiah sinning when he curses the day of his birth and accuses God of deceiving him?

views.label: Genuine, if extreme, lament within faith
views.case: Scripture records such raw complaint elsewhere, Job 3, several Psalms, as an accepted form of honest address to God from within a real relationship of faith, not a final verdict on God's character.

views.label: A real spiritual low point that Scripture records honestly
views.case: The text does not commend every word Jeremiah says here as theologically precise; it simply records, without editorial correction, how crushing the prophetic calling became, letting readers see that even faithful servants of God can reach real despair.

debates.bearing: Either way, Jeremiah does not stop prophesying, verse 13 breaks into praise, and the passage as a whole shows that honest anguish and continued faithfulness are not mutually exclusive.

study.originalLanguages: The jar Jeremiah breaks, baqbuq, is already hardened clay, unlike the soft, reworkable clay of the potter's wheel, marking a shift from warning to a judgment now fixed. Pashhur's new name, Magor-missabib, terror on every side, turns his own violence into a prophecy against himself, and the identical phrase later names the whispering campaign against Jeremiah. Underneath his suffering runs the driving image of esh, fire, God's word burning in his bones so that he cannot hold it in even when he most wants to.

typology.type: The jar shattered beyond repair, Jeremiah 19:10-11
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the New Testament's warning of a hardness of heart that becomes settled beyond repentance, Hebrews 6:4-6, while also underscoring, by contrast, the mercy still extended wherever people do turn.

typology.type: Jeremiah mocked and struck for speaking God's true word, Jeremiah 20:2, 7-8
typology.fulfillment: Prefigures Christ, mocked and struck for the very truth He spoke, Matthew 27:29-31, who endured the fuller measure of what Jeremiah suffered in shadow.

study.christ: Jeremiah's cry that he has become 'a laughingstock all day long' for faithfully speaking God's word anticipates the mockery Christ endured for the same reason, struck and derided by those who would not receive the truth. Where Jeremiah's anguish drives him to curse his own birth, Christ's anguish in Gethsemane drives Him instead to full submission, 'not My will but Yours be done,' fulfilling and surpassing the pattern of faithful suffering Jeremiah lived out under far greater strain than any prophet before him.
study.god: God's patience with Judah is real but not infinite, pictured starkly in a jar that, once broken, cannot be reshaped like the potter's clay before it. Yet God does not abandon His suffering servant: even as Jeremiah pours out despair bordering on accusation, God remains, in Jeremiah's own words, 'a fearsome warrior' at his side, present in the anguish rather than only after it.

crossReferences.note: Describes a hardness of heart that becomes settled and resistant to repentance, echoing the unmendable broken jar.
Job likewise curses the day of his birth, showing Jeremiah's lament stands within an accepted biblical tradition of raw complaint before God.
Christ is mocked and struck for the truth He spoke, the fuller pattern of what Jeremiah suffered here.
Encourages believers that suffering for righteousness is something Christ Himself shared, and is no strange thing.

study.oneStory: The unmendable jar and Jeremiah's anguished confession show how costly it is to carry God's true word into a world that will not hear it, sometimes to the point of despair that dares to curse one's own birth. That costly faithfulness is carried to its fullest and final measure in Christ, mocked and struck for speaking truth all the way to the cross, so that all who suffer for His name suffer within a story that has already been won.

quiz.q: How does the broken jar in chapter 19 differ from the clay at the potter's wheel in chapter 18?

options.text: The jar is already hardened, fired clay that, once shattered, can never be reworked, unlike the soft clay before.
options.note: This marks a deliberate escalation from a warning that could still be reshaped to a judgment now fixed.

options.text: There is no meaningful difference between the two images.
options.note: The text specifically notes the jar 'can never again be repaired,' unlike the reworkable clay.

options.text: The jar is softer than the potter's clay and easily reshaped.
options.note: The opposite is true; the jar is hardened, not softer.

options.text: The jar represents only the pottery trade, with no theological meaning.
options.note: The prophecy explicitly interprets the jar as picturing judgment on the nation.

quiz.q: Why does Jeremiah rename Pashhur 'Magor-missabib', terror on every side?

options.text: As a prophecy that Pashhur's own violence would return upon him and his household in captivity and death.
options.note: The name turns Pashhur's cruelty into a declaration of his coming fate.

options.text: Because Pashhur had shown Jeremiah great kindness.
options.note: Pashhur had Jeremiah beaten and placed in the stocks; the name is judgment, not honor.

options.text: It was simply Pashhur's family name already.
options.note: The text presents it as a new name Jeremiah gives him in response to his violence.

options.text: It described the location of the temple courtyard.
options.note: The name is about Pashhur's person and fate, not a place.

quiz.q: What does Jeremiah mean by describing God's word as 'a fire shut up in my bones'?

options.text: A compulsion to speak God's word that he cannot suppress even when persecution makes him want to stop.
options.note: Verse 9 shows Jeremiah trying to hold the message in, but being unable to bear the pressure of it.

options.text: A literal physical illness Jeremiah was suffering from.
options.note: This is figurative language for the compulsion of his prophetic calling, not a medical description.

options.text: His anger at Pashhur specifically, with no wider meaning.
options.note: The fire describes his relationship to God's message generally, not merely personal anger at one man.

options.text: A momentary feeling that quickly passed and did not return.
options.note: The imagery describes an ongoing, weary struggle, not something brief.

quiz.q: How does the chapter handle Jeremiah's raw, anguished prayer, including cursing his own birth?

options.text: It records his honest anguish without editorial correction, while showing he still returns to praising God and proclaiming His word.
options.note: Verse 13 breaks into praise even amid the lament, showing honesty and faithfulness together.

options.text: It shows Jeremiah abandoning his prophetic calling entirely after this prayer.
options.note: Jeremiah continues to prophesy in later chapters; this is a crisis, not an ending.

options.text: It presents the prayer as a model liturgical formula for regular worship.
options.note: This is personal, unfiltered lament, not a prescribed pattern for corporate worship.

options.text: It condemns Jeremiah explicitly for his words in this prayer.
options.note: The text records the prayer without an explicit condemnation, in keeping with the tradition of honest lament seen elsewhere in Scripture.

quiz.q: How does Jeremiah's suffering for speaking God's true word anticipate Christ?

options.text: Both were mocked and struck for speaking truth, though Christ's suffering fulfills and surpasses Jeremiah's in bearing the world's sin.
options.note: Jeremiah's mockery previews, in shadow, the far greater suffering Christ endured for the truth He spoke.

options.text: There is no meaningful connection between Jeremiah's suffering and Christ's.
options.note: The New Testament pattern of righteous suffering for truth directly echoes passages like this one.

options.text: Jeremiah's suffering was worse than Christ's in every respect.
options.note: Scripture presents Christ's suffering, bearing the sin of the world, as the fuller and greater fulfillment.

options.text: The connection is only that both men lived in the ancient Near East.
options.note: The connection is theological, mockery and suffering for faithfully speaking God's truth, not merely historical setting.

questions: The jar, once broken, could not be repaired. Is there an area of your life where you have let hardness set in that you need to bring to God now, before it becomes fixed?
Pashhur used his position of religious authority to silence Jeremiah rather than heed him. Where might you be tempted to silence an uncomfortable truth rather than examine it honestly?
Jeremiah could not hold in God's word even when it cost him dearly, calling it 'a fire shut up in my bones.' What is God calling you to say or do that you have been suppressing out of fear?
Jeremiah brought his rawest despair honestly to God rather than hiding it. Where do you need to bring your own honest anguish to God rather than pretending it is not there?
Even in his darkest prayer, Jeremiah still breaks into 'Sing to the LORD! Praise the LORD!' How might praise and honest lament coexist in your own walk with God this week?

translationNotes.note: The word for 'jar', baqbuq, is an onomatopoeic Hebrew word imitating the gurgling sound of liquid poured from a narrow-necked flask; some versions render it 'flask' or 'bottle'.
Magor-missabib, 'terror on every side,' is a Hebrew phrase that appears again in Jeremiah's own confession, verse 10, linking Pashhur's fate to the very persecution Jeremiah himself experiences.

This closing lament has strong parallels to Job 3:1-10; both passages curse the day of birth as an expression of extreme anguish, a recognized form of honest complaint within Israel's wisdom and prophetic tradition, not a doctrinal statement about the value of life.
